articles.title: Understand what changes with the mandatory Enamed to work as a doctor in Brazil
articles.summary: Starting in 2026, students entering medical school in Brazil must demonstrate proficiency in the Enamed (National Medical Training Assessment Examination) during their final year of study to obtain professional registration. This requirement is mandatory for all new medical students and applies specifically to the Enamed exam as a condition for licensure.
articles.insight:
This policy shift may indirectly influence Latin American student demand for medical degrees abroad, as heightened domestic licensing requirements could increase interest in internationally accredited programmes—though actual enrolment impact remains uncertain without data on cross-border qualification recognition or migration intent.  
Admissions teams should monitor Brazilian regulatory updates and student inquiries about licensure pathways, while assessing whether their medical programmes align with Enamed’s competencies or offer articulation routes to Brazilian registration, particularly for applicants from Brazil and neighbouring countries.
